name: Abdullah ibn Abdullah ibn Ubayy
epithet: The Son Who Chose the Prophet
title: Abdullah ibn Abdullah ibn Ubayy (RA): The Son Who Chose the Prophet
meta_description: The story of Abdullah ibn Abdullah ibn Ubayy (RA), a devoted companion of the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ whose own father was the chief of the hypocrites in Medina. A life of loyalty, restraint, and mercy.
keywords: Abdullah ibn Abdullah ibn Ubayy, son of Abdullah ibn Ubayy, story of a sahabi, companions of the Prophet, hypocrites of Medina, sahaba stories
lede: Imagine being raised to inherit a city, and watching your own father become the most dangerous man in it. Imagine loving that father, and loving even more the Prophet your father was working to destroy.

This is the story of Abdullah ibn Abdullah ibn Ubayy (may Allah be pleased with him): the son of the chief of the hypocrites of Medina, who gave his loyalty, his body, and his heart to the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ.

paras: His father was Abdullah ibn Ubayy, the chief of the hypocrites in Medina. A significant part of the Quran is devoted to the evil and corruption of this man and to all that he managed to stir. He was the one who got people to abandon the Prophet ﷺ in battle, who tried to spread division during the siege of the Trench, who fed the slander against the Prophet's own household. It was one harm after another, from within.

Before the Prophet ﷺ arrived, this was effectively the royal family of the city. The father expected to be crowned king of Yathrib, and the son was being groomed as the prince who would one day succeed him on the throne. By the measure of his world, the boy had everything to gain by following his father and everything to lose by leaving his path.

The son carried his father's name. The Prophet ﷺ gave him a new one: Abdullah, the servant of Allah. From the very beginning, his name announced the choice he had made. He would not be the heir of a hypocrite. He would be a servant of his Lord.

When Islam reached Medina, Abdullah did not hesitate. He embraced it sincerely and dedicated himself to the Prophet ﷺ without reservation. There was no slow softening, no waiting to see which way the city would turn. He simply believed, and stayed.

He was present at Badr, and not everyone among the Ansar was. Those who fought at Badr were a small and honored group, and he was one of them. He stayed firm at the Trench when others wavered. He took the pledge under the tree at Hudaybiyyah. He was there, again and again, in exactly the moments that asked the most of a person and offered the least reward in this world.

He was also one of the few who could read and write. The boy who had been prepared to rule instead became one of the scribes of the Prophet ﷺ. Some accounts say he was among those who wrote down the Quran: the son of the chief hypocrite, recording the very verses that exposed his father's plotting.
He did not only serve with his pen. Fighting alongside the Prophet ﷺ, he lost part of his nose and two of his teeth.

The Prophet ﷺ permitted him to replace the lost part of his nose with gold, and his two teeth with gold as well, because that was the treatment that held the repair together. Scholars later took from this very case the permissibility of using gold to set right what the body has lost. So he carried gold on his face for the rest of his life, a quiet record of where he had stood and what it had cost him.
The Prophet ﷺ trusted him with more than tasks. He appointed him as a commander in battle. While his father worked from within to weaken the Prophet ﷺ, the son was given authority and earned it.

It is worth pausing on how strange his life must have felt from the inside. The father was doing everything he could to push people away from the Prophet ﷺ and to weaken him from within. The son was among the most reliable men around the Prophet ﷺ, proving over and over that he would be there at his side.

When the father got people to flee in battle, the son was one of those who stayed. When the father stirred slander and division in the city, the son carried the Prophet's letters and wrote his words. The same household produced both the deepest wound and one of the steadiest hands.

He never let his father's reputation become his own excuse. He could have hidden behind his name, or grown bitter, or kept one foot in each camp to protect his future. He did none of that. He simply decided, early and clearly, where he belonged, and stayed there for the rest of his life.

It would have been easy to assume that a poisoned tree could only bear poisoned fruit. His life says otherwise. A person is not sentenced to the choices of the house they were born into. The same roof that produced the chief of the hypocrites also produced one of the most trusted of the believers.

sections.h2: A father's treason, a son's restraint
paras: On one journey his father said the words that crossed every line: that once they returned to Medina, the honored one would drive out the humiliated one, meaning that he himself would expel the Prophet ﷺ. It was treason of the highest order, and the punishment in any society of the time would have been death. Allah recorded the boast:

Word reached Abdullah that his father might be executed. The expectation in any society would have been simple: the man had been caught in open treason, and he would pay for it with his life. So the son came to the Prophet ﷺ, and what he said is one of the most striking things a son has ever said. He did not ask for mercy. He made a request that almost no one expects.

He said that no son loved his father more than he did, and that he had always been obedient to him, always honored him, always treated him well. This was not the bitterness of a child who hated his parent. But he feared that if someone else were ordered to carry out the execution, he would not be able to bear the sight of the man who had killed his father. He did not know whether he could ever forgive that man.

And so he was honest about the danger inside himself. He feared his own grief and pride might rise up, that the old ignorance might overtake him, and that he might one day raise his hand against that man and end up killing a believer while telling himself he was defending his father's honor. To close that door before it could open, he said: if my father must be put to death, command me to do it.

ayah.text: They say, 'Once we return to Medina the powerful will drive out the weak,' but power belongs to God, to His Messenger, and to the believers, though the hypocrites do not know this.

sections.h2: Treat him with the best of manners
paras: The Prophet ﷺ refused. There would be no execution, and certainly not by the son's hand. He told Abdullah that they would treat his father with the best of manners and keep good company with him for as long as he remained among them.

And he told the son something a son needed to hear: that is still your father, so treat him well. The Prophet ﷺ had not come to turn a child against his parent, even a parent like this one. The loyalty Abdullah owed to the Prophet ﷺ did not cancel the duty he owed to the man who raised him. He could hold both at once, and he was being asked to.

Later, when the father tried to enter Medina after his boast, it was his own son standing at the gates who stopped him. Abdullah told him plainly that he would not let him pass until he admitted who was truly honored and who was truly humbled. The son drew the line his father never would.

sections.h2: The shirt, the prayer, and the limit of mercy
paras: This is where the story shows its deepest human complication. When Abdullah ibn Ubayy died, his son was devastated. Whatever his father had been to the city, he was still a father to his son, and the grief was real. He came to the Prophet ﷺ and asked for the Prophet's own shirt to use as his father's burial cloth. The Prophet ﷺ took it off and gave it to him.

Then the son asked the Prophet ﷺ to come and pray over his father and to seek forgiveness for him. This was the man who had slandered the Prophet ﷺ, who had tried to overthrow him on more than one occasion, who had worked to destabilize the city from the very start. The Prophet ﷺ went out to lead the funeral prayer anyway, and someone tried to stop him: you would pray for this man, after everything he did?

That is when Allah revealed that even if the Prophet ﷺ were to seek forgiveness for him seventy times, it would not be granted, because the man had never truly believed. The verse drew a line that mercy itself could not cross. Yet the Prophet ﷺ had still answered a grieving son with his own shirt and his own presence.

ayah.text: It makes no difference [Prophet] whether you ask forgiveness for them or not: God will not forgive them even if you ask seventy times, because they reject God and His Messenger. God does not guide those who rebel against Him.

lessons_intro: His story is full of twists and turns, but underneath them all is a single, steady choice: he stayed where the truth was, even when the line ran straight through his own family. A few things he leaves us are worth carrying into an ordinary life.

lessons.text: He was born to inherit a throne. He chose to be a servant beside the Prophet ﷺ instead, and never used his father's name as an excuse for anything.
He gave his whole heart to the Prophet ﷺ and still loved and honored his father. He was not asked to hate one in order to follow the other.

lessons.bold: Know your own heart, and guard it.
lessons.text: He did not trust his own anger. He feared what grief might make him do, and arranged things to keep himself from a sin he had not yet committed.

lessons.bold: Mercy can be given even where it is not deserved.
lessons.text: The Prophet ﷺ answered a hostile man's funeral with his own shirt and his own presence, simply because a grieving son had asked.

faq.q: Who was Abdullah ibn Abdullah ibn Ubayy?
faq.a: A sincere and devoted companion of the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ from among the Ansar of Medina. He was the son of Abdullah ibn Ubayy, the chief of the hypocrites, yet he himself was loyal to the Prophet ﷺ from the beginning.

faq.q: Why did the Prophet change his name to Abdullah?
faq.a: His original name was changed by the Prophet ﷺ to Abdullah, meaning the servant of Allah. The new name reflected the faith he had chosen, in contrast to the path of his father.

faq.q: Did he really offer to execute his own father?
faq.a: When he heard his father might be put to death for treason, he asked that he be the one to carry it out, so that no other man would do it and leave him unable to forgive. The Prophet ﷺ refused and chose to treat the father with kindness instead.

faq.q: What can we learn from his life?
faq.a: That truth can be chosen over family pride, that loyalty to the truth does not require cruelty to those we love, and that we should guard our own hearts against anger before it leads us into sin.

closing_heading: Why this story stays with us
closing: Most of us are not asked to choose between a crown and the truth. But many of us are quietly pulled between what is right and the people we are tied to by blood, habit, or loyalty. Abdullah shows that it is possible to hold both honesty and tenderness at once: to refuse what is wrong without abandoning the person who is wrong, and to keep honoring a parent even while refusing to follow him.

His deepest fear was not his father. It was his own heart, and what grief and pride might do to it if he was not careful. That is a fear worth borrowing. Today, ask where your loyalties are really pointed, and ask Allah to keep your heart soft toward people and firm toward the truth at the same time. That balance is hard to hold for even a single day, and it is exactly what his life was made of.
source_note: The narrative follows the account of Abdullah ibn Abdullah ibn Ubayy (RA) in Dr. Omar Suleiman's series The Firsts (Yaqeen Institute). Quran translations are from The Qur'an, M.A.S. Abdel Haleem (Quran 63:8 and 9:80). Where the historical record carries more than one narration, we have followed the most widely reported.

slides:

A large part of the Quran was sent down about the harm he caused.

His son chose the Prophet ﷺ instead.

He was being groomed to be the prince of the city.

Instead he became a servant beside the Prophet ﷺ.

The Prophet ﷺ even changed his name to Abdullah: the servant of Allah.

He was at Badr. He held firm at the Trench. He took the pledge at Hudaybiyyah.

He could read and write, so he became a scribe, recording the very verses that exposed his father.

Fighting beside the Prophet ﷺ, he lost part of his nose and two teeth.

He replaced them with gold, and carried that gold for the rest of his life.

His father committed treason, and the punishment was death.

His son came to the Prophet ﷺ with an impossible request: if my father must be executed, let me be the one to do it.

He explained: no son loves his father more than I do.

But if another man kills him, I fear I could not forgive that man, and my anger might lead me to sin.

The Prophet ﷺ refused. No execution.

"That is still your father," he said. "Treat him well."

Loyalty to the truth did not mean cruelty to his family.

When the father died, the son asked for the Prophet's shirt as a burial cloth, and for a prayer over him.

The Prophet ﷺ gave both.

**Be firm toward what is wrong, and gentle toward the people in it.**

May Allah be pleased with him.
social.caption:
His father was the chief of the hypocrites in Medina, the man behind much of the harm recorded in the Quran. His son, Abdullah ibn Abdullah ibn Ubayy (may Allah be pleased with him), chose the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ with his whole heart.

He fought at Badr, held firm at the Trench, and carried gold on his face from wounds taken beside the Prophet ﷺ. When his father committed treason, he did not ask for mercy. He asked to carry out the sentence himself, so that no other hand would do it and leave his own heart open to revenge. The Prophet ﷺ refused, and told him to keep honoring his father.

Which part of his story stayed with you? 🤍

Save this for later, and share it with someone who would love it.

social.alt_caption: He was raised to inherit a city. He chose to be a servant of Allah instead.

email.subject: The son of the chief hypocrite chose the Prophet
email.alt_subject: "That is still your father. Treat him well."
email.preview: He offered to carry out his own father's execution, for a reason almost no one expects.
email.body:
Assalamu alaikum,

Imagine being raised to inherit a city, and watching your own father become the most dangerous man in it.

His father was Abdullah ibn Ubayy, the chief of the hypocrites in Medina. A large part of the Quran was sent down about the harm this man caused: the division he stirred, the slander he spread, the way he pulled people away from the Prophet ﷺ.

His son, also named Abdullah (may Allah be pleased with him), chose the other path completely. He embraced Islam sincerely. He was at Badr. He held firm at the Trench. He took the pledge at Hudaybiyyah. Raised to be a prince, he became a humble scribe of the Prophet ﷺ instead, even writing down the verses that exposed his own father.

Then came the hardest moment. His father committed open treason, and the punishment of the time was death. So the son came to the Prophet ﷺ with a request almost no one expects. He did not beg for mercy. He said: if my father must be executed, let me be the one to do it.

Not out of anger. He explained that no son loved his father more than he did. But he feared that if another man carried out the sentence, he would never be able to look at that man again, and his grief might push him into sin, into harming a believer to defend his father's honor.

The Prophet ﷺ refused. There would be no execution. And he told the son something gentle and important: that is still your father, so treat him well. Loyalty to the truth did not require him to be cruel to the man who raised him.

Later, when the father died, the son asked for the Prophet's own shirt as a burial cloth, and asked him to pray over him. The Prophet ﷺ gave both, even to a man who had spent years working against him.

There is a quiet lesson here that is easy to miss. We are rarely asked to choose between a throne and the truth. But we are often pulled between what is right and the people we love. Abdullah held both: firm toward the truth, gentle toward his father, and watchful over his own heart most of all.

May Allah be pleased with him, and keep our hearts soft toward people and firm toward what is right.

If you would like the full story, we have written it here:

paras: Imagine being raised to inherit a city, and watching your own father become the most dangerous man in it. His father was the chief of the hypocrites of Medina, and a large part of the Quran was sent down about the harm he caused.

His son, Abdullah ibn Abdullah ibn Ubayy (may Allah be pleased with him), made the opposite choice. He embraced Islam sincerely and gave himself to the Prophet ﷺ. He was at Badr. He held firm at the Trench. He took the pledge under the tree at Hudaybiyyah.

He had been groomed to be the prince of the city. Instead he became a scribe for the Prophet ﷺ, and is said to have written down some of the very verses that exposed his father's plotting. Fighting beside the Prophet ﷺ, he lost part of his nose and two teeth, and replaced them with gold he would carry the rest of his life.

Then his father committed open treason, boasting that he would drive the Prophet ﷺ out of Medina. The punishment of the time was death. So the son came to the Prophet ﷺ with a request no one expected. He did not ask for mercy. He asked to carry out the sentence himself.

He explained that no son loved his father more than he did. But if another man killed his father, he feared he could never forgive that man, and that grief and pride might drive him to sin. If it must be done, he said, let it be my hand and not another's.

The Prophet ﷺ refused. There would be no execution. And he told the son something gentle: that is still your father, so treat him well. Loyalty to the truth did not mean cruelty to the man who raised him.

When the father died, the grieving son asked for the Prophet's own shirt as a burial cloth, and for a prayer over him. The Prophet ﷺ gave him both, even though Allah revealed that this man would not be forgiven. The mercy shown to a son outlasted everything the father had done.
storybook.pullquote: That is still your father. Treat him well.
storybook.reflection: Abdullah was not asked to hate his father in order to follow the truth. He was asked to be honest about what was wrong, and kind to the person who was wrong, and most of all to guard his own heart against the anger that grief can stir. That is a balance worth carrying: firm toward what is right, gentle toward the people caught in it.
storybook.source: His story follows the account in Dr. Omar Suleiman's series The Firsts.
